primary_meaning: panacea, a cure-all remedy proposed as a universal solution to all diseases or problems
secondary_meaning: in extended usage, any solution wrongly believed to solve all difficulties
usage_notes: Often negative context: 'ce n'est pas une panacée', 'simple panacée'. Used for medical and metaphorical solutions.
common_collocations: simple panacée, fausse panacée, panacée universelle, panacée miracle, loin d'être une panacée
synonyms: remède universel, élixir miraculeux, solution magique
antonyms: problème insoluble, mal sans remède, impasse
image_search_query: universal cure-all remedy miracle medicine potion solution
image_context: Alchemical or medical illustration of mysterious universal healing potion
example_1: La technologie n'est pas une panacée pour résoudre tous les problèmes sociaux.
example_1_translation: Technology is not a panacea for solving all social problems.
example_2: Chercher une panacée universelle en médecine moderne s'avère toujours naïf.
example_2_translation: Seeking a universal panacea in modern medicine always proves naive.
example_3: Aucune politique économique unique ne peut servir de panacée à la récession.
example_3_translation: No single economic policy can serve as a panacea for recession.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: pa-na-SE. Stress on final 'é'.
memory_hook: Panacée - from Greek 'panacea' (cure-all) - all-healing remedy from mythology
related_words: remède, élixir, solution, miracle, cure, thérapie
common_mistakes: Not 'pana-SEE'; keep French 'é' sound at end

primary_meaning: quintessence, the purest, most perfect, or most concentrated form of something
secondary_meaning: historically, the fifth essence or ethereal substance composing celestial bodies
usage_notes: Abstract concept. Often: 'quintessence de', 'la quintessence même de'. Elevated register.
common_collocations: quintessence de, la quintessence même de, quintessence pure, quintessence de l'art
synonyms: essence pure, perfection, distillation, apothéose, summum
image_search_query: pure essence distilled perfect refined philosophical concept crystalline
image_context: Alchemical illustration showing essence or pure distilled substance
example_1: La quintessence de la philosophie antique réside dans la quête du bonheur.
example_1_translation: The quintessence of ancient philosophy lies in the quest for happiness.
example_2: Ce tableau incarne la quintessence pure de l'art baroque.
example_2_translation: This painting embodies the quintessence of Baroque art.
example_3: Elle représente la quintessence même de l'élégance française.
example_3_translation: She represents the very quintessence of French elegance.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: kin-te-SANS. Nasal 'in' and 'an' sounds.
common_mistakes: Nasal vowels are key; avoid English 'kwin-'

primary_meaning: to recoil, draw back, or shrink in fear; to show aversion or repugnance
secondary_meaning: to retreat psychologically from a threat or unpleasant stimulus
usage_notes: Formal/literary. Regular verb conjugation. 'Recazir devant', 'recazir de'.
common_collocations: recazir devant, recazir de, recazir d'horreur, faire recazir
synonyms: reculer, se retirer, frémir, tressaillir, se dérober
image_search_query: fear response recoil shrink back aversion horror
image_context: Representation of person recoiling in fear or disgust
example_1: Devant le danger immédiat, le guerrier ne fit recazir ni ses compagnons.
example_1_translation: Facing immediate danger, the warrior made neither himself nor his companions recoil.
example_2: Elle recazit d'horreur à la vue du spectacle macabre.
example_2_translation: She recoiled in horror at the sight of the macabre spectacle.
example_3: Beaucoup recazissent devant l'idée même de l'engagement éternel.
example_3_translation: Many recoil at the very idea of eternal commitment.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: re-ka-ZIR. Stress on final syllable.
memory_hook: Recazir - verb expressing instinctive withdrawal from danger or unpleasantness
related_words: reculer, se retirer, frémir, tressaillir, horreur
common_mistakes: Don't confuse with 'reculer' (step back); 'recazir' emphasizes emotional response

primary_meaning: synecdoche, a figure of speech where part represents whole or vice versa
secondary_meaning: a rhetorical device expressing relationship through substitution of names
usage_notes: Literary/rhetorical analysis. Often: 'synecdoque de partie pour le tout' or reverse.
common_collocations: synecdoque rhétorique, synecdoque de partie, synecdoque du tout, procédé de synecdoque
synonyms: figure de rhétorique, métonymie, substitution, transfert de sens
image_search_query: rhetoric part representing whole synecdoche literary device
image_context: Illustration showing part of object representing whole concept
example_1: Dire 'voiles' pour désigner les 'navires' constitue une synecdoque classique.
example_1_translation: Saying 'sails' for 'ships' constitutes a classic synecdoche.
example_2: La synecdoque 'tous les âges' remplace implicitement 'gens de tous les âges'.
example_2_translation: The synecdoche 'all ages' implicitly replaces 'people of all ages'.
example_3: Les poètes modernes utilisent la synecdoque pour créer des effets de compression sémantique.
example_3_translation: Modern poets use synecdoche to create semantic compression effects.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: si-NEK-do-k. Stress on second syllable.
related_words: métonymie, métaphore, rhétorique, figure de style, trope
common_mistakes: Confuse with 'métonymie'; synecdoche is part-whole, métonymie is part-part

primary_meaning: teratogenic, capable of causing malformations or birth defects in a fetus
secondary_meaning: relating to substances or conditions that increase risk of developmental abnormalities
usage_notes: Medical/scientific register. Modifies nouns: 'substance tératogène', 'effet tératogène'.
common_collocations: substance tératogène, effet tératogène, risque tératogène, potentiel tératogène, produit tératogène
image_search_query: birth defect malformation embryo development fetal abnormality
image_context: Medical illustration of fetal development showing teratogenic effects
example_1: Cette substance est reconnue comme tératogène depuis plusieurs décennies.
example_1_translation: This substance has been recognized as teratogenic for several decades.
example_2: Les effets tératogènes de ce médicament ont entraîné son retrait immédiat du marché.
example_2_translation: The teratogenic effects of this drug led to its immediate withdrawal from market.
example_3: L'exposition tératogène pendant le premier trimestre pose des risques majeurs.
example_3_translation: Teratogenic exposure during the first trimester poses major risks.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: te-ra-to-JEN. Stress on final syllable.
common_mistakes: Not 'tera-togen'; French keeps stress on final 'gène'

secondary_meaning: philosophically, existing in all places at all times
common_collocations: présence ubiquiste, surveillance ubiquiste, nature ubiquiste, technologie ubiquiste
image_search_query: omnipresence everywhere simultaneously present ubiquitous pervasive
image_context: Abstract visualization of presence everywhere at once
example_1: La présence ubiquiste de la technologie dans notre vie quotidienne est indéniable.
example_1_translation: The ubiquitous presence of technology in our daily life is undeniable.
example_2: L'omniprésence ubiquiste des réseaux sociaux transforme les structures sociales.
example_2_translation: The ubiquitous omnipresence of social networks transforms social structures.
example_3: Cette surveillance ubiquiste menace les libertés individuelles fondamentales.
example_3_translation: This ubiquitous surveillance threatens fundamental individual freedoms.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: u-bi-KIS-t. Stress on third syllable. Nasal 'u' opening.
common_mistakes: Not anglicized 'yoo-'BIK-wi-tus'; French keeps closed 'u' sound

primary_meaning: vicariance, the splitting of a population's range into isolated geographic areas leading to speciation
secondary_meaning: in biogeography, the evolutionary divergence of closely related species in isolated regions
usage_notes: Scientific/biological context. Often: 'vicariance biogéographique', 'événement de vicariance'.
common_collocations: vicariance biogéographique, événement de vicariance, processus de vicariance, modèle de vicariance
synonyms: spéciation allopatrique, isolement géographique, divergence allopatrique
image_search_query: geographic isolation separated populations speciation biogeography continents
image_context: Map showing separated landmasses and isolated populations representing vicariance
example_1: La vicariance biogéographique explique la distribution de formes soeurs sur continents séparés.
example_1_translation: Biogeographical vicariance explains sister species distribution on separated continents.
example_2: La vicariance a permis la spéciation de populations isolées sur des îles distinctes.
example_2_translation: Vicariance enabled speciation of isolated populations on distinct islands.
example_3: Cet événement de vicariance s'est produit il y a environ 15 millions d'années.
example_3_translation: This vicariance event occurred approximately 15 million years ago.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: vi-ka-RIANS. Stress on final syllable. Nasal 'an'.
memory_hook: Vicariance - from Latin 'vicarius' (substitute, replacing) - populations replacing each other geographically
related_words: spéciation, allopatrique, isolement géographique, biogeographie, endemic
common_mistakes: Not 'vee-car-'; keep short 'i' and stress on final syllable

primary_meaning: warrant, a legal document authorizing an officer to make an arrest, seizure, or search
secondary_meaning: in commerce, a guarantee or certificate of authority to sell goods
usage_notes: Legal/commercial jargon borrowed from English. Often: 'warrant d'arrêt', 'warrant de perquisition'.
common_collocations: warrant d'arrêt, warrant de perquisition, warrant bancaire, warrant d'appel
image_search_query: legal warrant document official authorization seal judicial
image_context: Official legal document with seals and signatures representing warrant
example_1: Sans warrant officiel, toute perquisition serait illégale et irrecevable.
example_1_translation: Without an official warrant, any search would be illegal and inadmissible.
example_2: Le warrant bancaire garantissait le prêt accordé à l'entreprise en difficulté.
example_2_translation: The bank warrant guaranteed the loan granted to the struggling business.
example_3: Le juge émit un warrant d'arrestation contre le suspect identifié.
example_3_translation: The judge issued an arrest warrant against the identified suspect.
pronunciation_tips: One syllable: wa-RAN. English 'war-' sound, French 'an' ending.
memory_hook: Warrant - English loanword used in French legal contexts for official authorization
related_words: mandat, autorisation, document, acte officiel, légalité
common_mistakes: Keep English pronunciation but add French nasal 'an'

primary_meaning: xenophobic, fearful of or hostile to foreigners and foreign culture
secondary_meaning: relating to discrimination or prejudice against people from other countries
usage_notes: Can be adjective (m/f) or noun. Often: 'discours xénophobe', 'politique xénophobe', 'personne xénophobe'.
common_collocations: discours xénophobe, politique xénophobe, attitudes xénophobes, campagne xénophobe
synonyms: hostile aux étrangers, raciste, discriminatoire, intolérant
image_search_query: xenophobia fear foreigners prejudice discrimination borders wall
image_context: Symbolic representation of fear and prejudice toward foreign peoples
example_1: Les discours xénophobes sont en hausse dans plusieurs pays européens.
example_1_translation: Xenophobic speeches are increasing in several European countries.
example_2: La politique xénophobe du gouvernement fut largement critiquée par l'opposition.
example_2_translation: The government's xenophobic policy was widely criticized by the opposition.
example_3: Ces attitudes xénophobes reflètent une peur infondée de l'altérité.
example_3_translation: These xenophobic attitudes reflect unfounded fear of otherness.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: kse-no-FOB. Stress on final syllable. Silent 'k' at start.
memory_hook: Xénophobe - from Greek 'xenos' (foreigner) + 'phobos' (fear) - fear of foreigners
common_mistakes: Initial 'x' pronounced 'ks' not 'z'; maintain stress on 'phobe'

primary_meaning: yeoman, a free farmer of small means in medieval and early modern England
secondary_meaning: a person of middling social status; a loyal and reliable worker
usage_notes: Historical/literary context. Plural is 'yeomen' in English but often 'yeomans' in French usage.
common_collocations: yeoman anglais, classe des yeomen, yeoman paysan, classe yeoman
synonyms: fermier libre, paysan propriétaire, homme de condition moyenne
image_search_query: medieval English farmer yeoman rural peasant free landowner
image_context: Historical depiction of English yeoman farmer with tools and land
example_1: Le yeoman anglais représentait la colonne vertébrale de la société rurale médiévale.
example_1_translation: The English yeoman represented the backbone of medieval rural society.
example_2: Les yeomen ont joué un rôle crucial dans les guerres civiles anglaises du 17e siècle.
example_2_translation: Yeomen played a crucial role in English civil wars of the 17th century.
example_3: Cette classe de yeomen intermédiaire évita à l'Angleterre l'extrême polarisation.
example_3_translation: This intermediate class of yeomen prevented England from extreme polarization.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: JO-man. Final 'n' nasal. English loanword.
memory_hook: Yeoman - Old English 'ge-man' (retainer/man) - free farmer of middling rank
related_words: fermier, paysan, propriétaire foncier, classe moyenne, féodalité
common_mistakes: Historical term; avoid using for modern farmers

primary_meaning: zetetic, relating to skeptical inquiry and critical examination without accepting authority
secondary_meaning: a method of questioning and testing claims through systematic skepticism
usage_notes: Philosophy/academic context. Often: 'approche zététique', 'méthode zététique', 'enquête zététique'.
common_collocations: approche zététique, méthode zététique, enquête zététique, analyse zététique
image_search_query: skeptical inquiry questioning philosophy critical thinking investigation
image_context: Philosopher or investigator questioning evidence and assumptions
example_1: La méthode zététique consiste à questionner toutes les affirmations sans exception.
example_1_translation: The zetetic method consists of questioning all claims without exception.
example_2: L'approche zététique favorise l'examen critique des croyances établies.
example_2_translation: The zetetic approach favors critical examination of established beliefs.
example_3: Cette enquête zététique révéla l'infondé de nombreuses accusations.
example_3_translation: This zetetic inquiry revealed the baselessness of many accusations.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: ze-te-TIK. Stress on final syllable.
memory_hook: Zététique - from Greek 'zetetikos' (inquiring) - seeking truth through questioning
common_mistakes: Not Greek but French adaptation; keep final stress

primary_meaning: acme, the highest point or crisis of a disease; the peak intensity
secondary_meaning: in general usage, the highest point of achievement or intensity
usage_notes: Medical/formal register. Often: 'acmé de la maladie', 'atteindre son acmé'.
common_collocations: acmé de la maladie, acmé de la crise, atteindre son acmé, au acmé de
synonyms: apogée, sommet, point culminant, paroxysme, zénith
image_search_query: peak crisis highest point disease fever summit climax
image_context: Graph or medical chart showing peak intensity of disease or crisis
example_1: La fièvre atteignit son acmé au troisième jour de la maladie infectieuse.
example_1_translation: The fever reached its acme on the third day of the infectious illness.
example_2: L'acmé de la crise financière survint lors de l'effondrement de 2008.
example_2_translation: The acme of the financial crisis occurred during the 2008 collapse.
example_3: Ce poète atteignit l'acmé de son art dans ses œuvres tardives.
example_3_translation: This poet reached the acme of his art in his later works.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: AK-me. Stress on first syllable. Final 'e' silent.
memory_hook: Acmé - from Greek 'akmé' (peak, highest point) - pinnacle of intensity
related_words: apogée, sommet, zénith, paroxysme, point culminant

primary_meaning: belligerence, a state of active warfare or hostile military action
secondary_meaning: aggressive or combative behavior or attitude
usage_notes: Formal/political register. Often: 'état de belligérance', 'suspendre la belligérance'.
common_collocations: état de belligérance, suspension de belligérance, acte de belligérance, cri de belligérance
image_search_query: warfare conflict hostile military combat aggression violence
image_context: War and conflict imagery representing military belligerence
example_1: La belligérance du pays envers ses voisins entraîna des sanctions internationales.
example_1_translation: The country's belligerence toward its neighbors led to international sanctions.
example_2: Les deux nations ont suspendu leur belligérance pour négocier une paix durable.
example_2_translation: The two nations suspended their belligerence to negotiate lasting peace.
example_3: Cet acte de belligérance constitua une violation flagrante du droit international.
example_3_translation: This act of belligerence constituted a flagrant violation of international law.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: be-li-JE-RANS. Stress on third syllable. Nasal 'an'.
memory_hook: Belligérance - from Latin 'bellum' (war) + '-gerence' (conduct) - conduct of war
common_mistakes: Not 'bell-'; keep soft 'e' sound at beginning

primary_meaning: cynorrhodon, the fruit of the wild rose (rosehip), rich in vitamin C
secondary_meaning: a medicinal plant used in traditional remedies
usage_notes: Botanical/medical context. Often: 'fruit de cynorrhodon', 'thé de cynorrhodon'.
common_collocations: fruit de cynorrhodon, thé de cynorrhodon, confiture de cynorrhodon, propriétés du cynorrhodon
image_search_query: rose hip wild rose fruit red medicinal plant
image_context: Red rosehips on wild rose bush representing cynorrhodon
example_1: Le cynorrhodon est particulièrement riche en vitamine C et possède des propriétés médicinales.
example_1_translation: Rose hip is particularly rich in vitamin C and has medicinal properties.
example_2: La confiture de cynorrhodon était traditionnellement utilisée pour traiter les rhumes.
example_2_translation: Rose hip jam was traditionally used to treat colds.
example_3: Le thé de cynorrhodon renforce le système immunitaire et prévient les infections.
example_3_translation: Rose hip tea strengthens the immune system and prevents infections.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: si-no-RO-don. Stress on third syllable.
related_words: églantier, rose sauvage, fruit, plante médicinale, herboristerie

primary_meaning: dysphonia, difficulty in producing voice; abnormal voice quality
secondary_meaning: any disorder affecting voice production and quality
common_collocations: dysphonie spasmodique, dysphonie fonctionnelle, dysphonie professionnelle, trouble dysphonique
synonyms: trouble vocal, enrouement, dysarthrie, problème de voix
antonyms: voix claire, voix saine, phonation normale
image_search_query: voice disorder dysphonia throat larynx vocal cord abnormality
image_context: Medical illustration of vocal cord disorders causing dysphonia
example_1: La dysphonie spasmodique affecte gravement la qualité et la clarté de la voix.
example_1_translation: Spasmodic dysphonia severely affects voice quality and clarity.
example_2: Les chanteurs professionnels sont souvent victimes de dysphonie occupationnelle.
example_2_translation: Professional singers are often victims of occupational dysphonia.
example_3: La dysphonie chronique peut indiquer des problèmes laryngés sérieux.
example_3_translation: Chronic dysphonia can indicate serious laryngeal problems.
pronunciation_tips: Three syllables: dis-fo-NI. Stress on final syllable.
